title: Cat or Kitten Straining to Defecate: Triage Guide and Next Steps
meta_desc: Is your kitten straining to poop? Learn about tenesmus, the medium urgency level of constipation, and how a photo can help your vet provide care.
content_html:
TL;DR: If your cat is straining to defecate, it may have constipation or a blockage requiring veterinary attention; however, ensure it isn't a life-threatening urinary emergency.

What does it mean if my cat is straining to defecate?

Straining to defecate, medically known as tenesmus, occurs when a cat or kitten frequently attempts to pass stool with little to no success. This behavior often looks like spending prolonged periods in the litter box or hunched over in a squatting position. It can be caused by various issues, including simple constipation, dietary indiscretion, intestinal parasites, or even a more serious blockage in the digestive tract.

Is it an emergency if my cat is straining to poop?

  • The urgency level for a cat straining to defecate for 24 hours is Medium, as it causes significant discomfort and can lead to complications.
  • Contact a veterinarian immediately if the straining is accompanied by vomiting, extreme lethargy, or a painful abdomen.
  • It is critical to ensure the cat is actually trying to defecate and not experiencing a life-threatening urinary blockage, which can look very similar.

How can a photo or video help a vet triage my cat?

  • A photo of the litter box or passed material allows the vet to see the color, consistency, and presence of any blood or mucus.
  • A short video of the cat's posture while straining can help distinguish between a gastrointestinal issue and a urinary emergency.
  • Visual evidence ensures your pet gets the correct type of urgent care quickly.

Clinical Context (Merck Veterinary Manual)

In cats or kittens straining to defecate, clinical evaluation should include palpation to determine the presence of perineal hernia, foreign material, pain, or masses. Abdominal radiographs may help identify inciting factors of fecal retention. Further diagnostics such as barium enema, ultrasonography, or colonoscopy may be necessary to demonstrate obstructive lesions or predisposing causes of chronic constipation, while a CBC, biochemical profile including a serum T4 level, urinalysis, and detailed neurologic examination should be considered in chronic or recurring cases. Underlying causes of constipation include narrowed pelvic inlet, enlarged sublumbar lymph nodes or prostate gland, colonic stricture, megacolon, hypothyroidism, dysautonomia, spinal cord lesions, hypokalemia, hypercalcemia, and certain medications (e.g., opioids, diuretics, antihistamines, anticholinergic agents). Treatment for mild constipation consists of adequate hydration, avoidance of dietary indiscretion, access to water, high-fiber diets, and suppository laxatives such as dioctyl sodium sulfosuccinate, glycerin, or bisacodyl, while moderate to recurrent constipation may require enemas (e.g., warm tap water, isotonic saline, DSS, mineral oil, or lactulose) or manual extraction of impacted feces.

Chapter: Gastroenterology

Source: The Merck Veterinary Manual, 11th Edition (Page 381)

faqs.question: Could my cat be straining due to a urinary issue instead of constipation?
faqs.answer: Yes, it is often difficult for owners to tell the difference. If a male cat is straining and not producing urine, this is a high-priority emergency.

faqs.question: Can I give my kitten milk or oil to help them poop?
faqs.answer: No, you should avoid giving home remedies like milk or mineral oil without veterinary guidance, as these can cause severe diarrhea or aspiration pneumonia.

faqs.question: When does constipation become a critical emergency?
faqs.answer: It becomes critical if the cat starts vomiting, stops eating entirely, or becomes extremely weak, as this may indicate a complete intestinal obstruction.
